So James, this ethnic unity and progress promotion law uh was introduced by the National People's Congress Ethnic Affairs Committee on September 8th, 2025 and it was signed into law and became effective July 1st, 2026. They haven't yet weaponized that, but that was a pretty expansive extr territorial insert they put into the new uh export restrictions on rare earths. But my my my take and the reason why I lump that in the category of this one is that in a way it's more about the symbolism that uh as opposed to being, you know, uh an offensive legislation that they're going to go out on um and to really try to find people and and stop them. I think it's more like case by case. Are they worried about a particular group or a particular country? Because remember a lot of other countries have agency. When I look back to some of the sanctions over the weer rights issues, labor rights issues, Chinese issued sanctions on on European ministers, uh, European Union also issued sanctions on Chinese companies and individuals in response. So it becomes a bit of a multipolar trap in the sense that you have escalation dominance or escalation potential on both sides. And I want to take it a little bit back maybe to the domestic side of things which I find interesting because as a historian a big part of what the CCP under Mao first tried to do is to really nationalize the system because if you recall uh China is a multi-thnic but also multi-electical country. You know my own people are from Shanghai. Shanghai is a very particular dialect. If anyone who's not living in Shanghai speaks to a Shanghai person, they can find it very difficult to understand them. And you know, very early on when the communists came to power, they enforced uh you know, unified writing system and simplified u made sure that schools use Mandarin as their primary language. And this law in in effect is trying to reinvigorate um I think some of those moves because you now it's saying that schools and government agencies must use Mandarin as their primary language and that curricula needs to uh quote unquote forge a strong sense of the community of the Chinese people. There's also an element of trying to make sure that people speak the same language and think the same way. Right? and and you know we talked about the extr territorial dimension but there's also this kind of internal unity dimension uh and it you know comes up in the education system in in the political system but I thought that that was another interesting aspect of this law it's hearkening back to the legacy of of of Mao >> yeah absolutely I think the last thing to say on this from my side would be that it has attracted criticism from abroad uh outside China I'll just mention a couple of those a UN letter from four eight former special reporters uh said earlier on this year that the law could violate at least 12 international human rights treaties that China has ratified. That's the first thing. And then the other piece of reaction has been from the European Parliament which adopted a resolution in April this year condemning this law. uh we're talking about the ethnic unity law uh and warning that it could intensify the systematic suppression of ethnic identities. That's a quotation and further strain uh relations between the EU and China. Of course, China has rejected those criticisms. Um so, you know, this is yet another point of tension uh between the West and China or is uh evolving into that. >> Yeah. And the last thing that I will have to say, James, you know, as much as this is a political issue, I have noticed, again, this is more of a cultural observation as a political one based on travel to China, that there is more of an opening up and a I would say from everyday people an appreciation of different dialects and different ethnic minority groups, a celebration of their cultures. You know, I was in Junan a couple uh two years ago, and it's the most dense area for ethnic minorities in China. You've got the mao, you got the hoy, you you've got a lot of different ethnic groups there and pe everyday people in China are keen to learn about their different cultural practices. So I just wanted to offer a flip side which is yes this is seems like a bit of a politically sensitive and uh potentially controversial issue but I have detected at least in everyday Chinese society that there still isn't a growing appreciation um for the ethnic minority groups and not just the HanchChinese identity. >> Yeah, I would I would definitely agree with that. I have personal friends who uh very much respect certain uh ethnic minority cultures um and religions in China and uh it I I I I very much hear from them exactly the same trend that you're talking about. >> All right, James, you know what time it is? It is prediction time. As you peer into the crystal ball for the future this week, what do you see? Okay, I'm going back to the big topic for me, which is uh the EU's commercial relationship with China. This is a massive topic. Uh let's not forget that the European Union comprises about 450 million uh consumers. Uh this is one of the biggest markets in the world and um EU exports to China in my prediction will continue to fall this year. In 2025 they were about 199.6 billion euro. This year I think they'll fall back to around 180 billion euro. So that's about a 10% contraction. And it contrasts with Chinese exports to Europe, which so far this year have been rising by around 16%. So you've got a a a very significant contraction of exports to China from Europe and a very significant increase uh of Chinese exports to Europe. So that's my prediction. Alice, what have you got for us? So I've been running numbers on the Chinese consumer landscape and what I find fascinating just looking at the recent history since co is that Chinese households are actually quite sensitive to what I would call geopolitical risk. So in a way they um attach a risk premier to their consumption based on the uncertainties that they're seeing geopolitically economically. We saw that first with a massive uh downfall in Chinese consumer confidence uh during the co lockdowns from 2020 really to 2022 on and then from 2022 onwards uh Russia Ukraine had a huge impact on Chinese consumer spending habits as well as as confidence and we're starting to see you know early signs that the Iran crisis is also starting to weigh on consumer confidence. So my my prediction, it's kind of unfair because I've already read the numbers, would be that uh we're going to see further weakening in Chinese consumer confidence and therefore spending because uh everyday Chinese people are worried about not just the geopolitical landscape but also the economic landscape in terms of uh hit to external demand. All right, that's all for this episode. Thank you for listening to China Decode. This is a production of ProfG Media. Make sure to follow us wherever you get your podcast so you don't miss an episode. and we'll talk to you again next week.
